name: Luarca - Esva Gorges Loop
name_original: Luarca a San Pedro de Paredes — ruta circular
description: This difficult 24.2-mile touring cycling route from Luarca to San Pedro de Paredes offers challenging climbs and diverse Asturian scenery.
region_intro: Touring cycling around Valdés offers routes through a unique landscape characterized by a dramatic, flat-topped promontory extending into the Atlantic. The region features a diverse shoreline with rocky cliffs, shallow bays, sand dunes, and extensive intertidal mudflats and sandy beaches. Inland, the terrain transitions to generally flat semi-desert scrubland, including shallow lakes and salt pans. This windswept expanse provides a distinct environment for cycling. Best touring cycling routes around Valdés The most popular touring cycling route is Luarca - Esva Gorges Loop, a 24.2 miles (39.0 km) trail that takes 3 hours 32 minutes to complete. This difficult route features significant elevation gain. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Cudillero coastal section – Luarca Fishing Village loop from Arcayana, a difficult 58.1 miles (93.5 km) path. This route offers a challenging long-distance ride. Local touring cyclists also love the Cudillero coastal section – Camino de Santiago loop from Caneiru, a 93.7 miles (150.9 km) trail leading through varied terrain, often completed in about 9 hours 54 minutes. Touring cycling around Valdés is defined by coastal sections, challenging climbs, and varied landscapes. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate to difficult. The routes in Valdés are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 89 reviews. More than 980 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Valdés's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in Valdés?
A: There are over 50 touring cycling routes around Valdés, offering a wide range of options for exploring the region's diverse landscapes. These routes are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.7 stars.

Q: What is the typical terrain like for touring cycling in Valdés?
A: The terrain in Valdés is characterized by varied landscapes, including dramatic coastlines with rocky cliffs, shallow bays, and beaches, as well as inland sections that can transition to more open, windswept areas. Many routes feature challenging climbs and descents, reflecting the region's rugged beauty.

Q: Are there any easy touring cycling routes for beginners in Valdés?
A: While many routes in Valdés are considered difficult, there are 5 easy touring cycling routes available. These routes offer a gentler introduction to the region's cycling opportunities, allowing beginners to enjoy the scenery at a more relaxed pace.

Q: What kind of natural features and landmarks can I expect to see along the cycling routes?
A: Valdés offers a wealth of natural features and landmarks. You can encounter stunning coastal views, such as those near the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3710397" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Luarca Lighthouse&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4999971" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vidio Lighthouse&lt;/a&gt;, and serene spots like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/93692" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Beach of Silence&lt;/a&gt;. Inland, you might discover hidden gems like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/865581" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Nonaya Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;. The region's diverse shoreline and semi-desert scrubland provide a unique backdrop for your rides.

Q: Are there any challenging long-distance routes for experienced touring cyclists?
A: Yes, Valdés is well-suited for experienced touring cyclists seeking a challenge. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/6971544" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cudillero coastal section – Luarca Fishing Village loop from Arcayana&lt;/a&gt;, spanning over 93 km, or the even longer &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40361384" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cudillero coastal section – Camino de Santiago loop from Caneiru&lt;/a&gt; (over 150 km), offer significant elevation gain and require good fitness.

Q: What are some notable towns or historical sites I can visit while cycling?
A: Beyond the natural beauty, you can explore charming settlements and historical sites. The town of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/353352" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Luarca&lt;/a&gt; is a popular highlight, as is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/363278" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Navia&lt;/a&gt;. Historical attractions include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/353344" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Selgas Palace and the Jesús Nazareno Church‑Pantheon&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5125287" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Collegiate Church of St. Mary Major in Salas&lt;/a&gt;, offering cultural stops along your journey.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in Valdés?
A: Many of the touring cycling routes in Valdés are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1029985557/luarca-esva-gorges-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Luarca - Esva Gorges Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the longer &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40324580" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cudillero coastal section – Luarca Fishing Village loop from Cadavedo&lt;/a&gt;, providing convenient options for your cycling adventures.

Q: What is the best time of year to go touring cycling in Valdés?
A: Given the region's windswept nature and diverse weather patterns, spring and autumn generally offer pleasant conditions for touring cycling, with milder temperatures and potentially less extreme winds. Summer can be warm, while winter might bring cooler, more unpredictable weather, especially along the exposed coastline.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Valdés?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 89 reviews. Reviewers often praise the challenging coastal sections, the varied landscapes, and the sense of adventure offered by the region's unique environment.

Q: Are there any routes that pass by significant religious or historical buildings?
A: Yes, several routes offer opportunities to see significant religious and historical buildings. You might pass by the charming &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/363182" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Regalina Chapel&lt;/a&gt;, the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5125287" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Collegiate Church of St. Mary Major in Salas&lt;/a&gt;, or the serene &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4506626" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of San Roque of Tineo&lt;/a&gt;, adding a cultural dimension to your ride.

Q: What should I pack for a touring cycling trip in Valdés?
A: Considering the windswept and potentially varied weather conditions, it's advisable to pack layers, including windproof and waterproof outer shells. Sun protection is also crucial due to open landscapes. For longer routes, carry sufficient water and snacks, as services might be sparse in some remote sections.
tips: It is worth visiting the La Vega dolmen, the Ovienes Menhir, the parish church of San Pedro, and the Hoces del Esva.

The Luarca lighthouse is located in the Watchtower, at Focicón point, closing the harbor inlet to the east, about three hundred meters from the town of whose urban layout it can be said that it is part. Chapel of the Watchtower, the cemetery and remains of the defensive wall from the 16th century. It dates from 1860 and consists of a house for the lighthouse keeper and a tower that is inserted into it on the west side, later new buildings are added on the east side.

This old fishing village is located at the mouth of the Rio Negro. The town squeezes into the valley dug by the river and seems to meander with it. In the town's harbor you can take a wonderful break and stock up on new provisions and water.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of the Luarca to San Pedro de Paredes circular route?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as difficult for touring cycling. It covers approximately 38.9 kilometers (24.2 miles) with a substantial elevation gain of about 1,308 meters (4,298 feet) and a similar descent. It requires very good physical fitness due to these significant elevation changes.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route primarily features mostly paved surfaces, making it suitable for touring bikes. However, despite being paved, the elevation profile includes challenging ramps and considerable vertical gain and loss as it navigates inland river valleys and gorges.

faqs.question: Where does the Luarca to San Pedro de Paredes circular route start?
faqs.answer: The route typically starts and ends in the charming fishing village of Luarca. You can find parking options within Luarca to begin your ride.

faqs.question: Are there parking options available in Luarca for cyclists?
faqs.answer: Yes, as Luarca serves as the starting and ending point for this circular route, you will find various parking options within the town suitable for cyclists.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery will I see along the route?
faqs.answer: Cyclists can expect diverse and picturesque Asturian landscapes. Starting in coastal Luarca, known for its unique river and rocky coast, the route transitions to inland vistas towards San Pedro de Paredes. You'll experience river valleys, lush forests, and dramatic geological formations around river gorges, characteristic of western Asturias.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the Luarca to San Pedro de Paredes circular route?
faqs.answer: Key points of interest include the starting and ending point, the charming fishing village of Luarca, and the village of San Pedro de Paredes. You'll also pass by the Luarca Lighthouse, offering coastal views.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle in Asturias, including this route, is generally during the spring (April-June) and autumn (September-October) months. During these periods, the weather is typically milder, and the landscapes are vibrant, avoiding the peak heat of summer and the colder, wetter conditions of winter.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this cycling route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle the Luarca to San Pedro de Paredes circular route itself. However, if you plan to visit specific attractions or natural parks along the way, it's always good to check their individual regulations.

faqs.question: Is the Luarca to San Pedro de Paredes circular route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route is on public roads and generally accessible, it is a demanding touring cycling route with significant elevation changes. Bringing a dog, especially one that would need to keep pace with a cyclist for nearly 39 km, is not recommended. If you plan to stop and explore areas on foot, always keep your dog on a leash and adhere to local regulations.

faqs.question: Does the route pass through the Hoces del Esva Natural Monument, and are there any specific regulations?
faqs.answer: The cycling route ventures into the general area inland from Luarca, where the landscape is characterized by river valleys and gorges, similar to the nearby Hoces del Esva Natural Monument. While the cycling route itself doesn't directly traverse the core hiking trails of Las Hoces del Esva, it offers views of similar rugged natural beauty. There are no specific entrance fees or permits required for cycling on the public roads that form this route. However, if you decide to explore the natural monument on foot, always respect protected areas, stay on marked paths, and follow any posted regulations regarding waste, noise, and wildlife.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route briefly intersects with sections of several other paths. Notably, it crosses parts of the Ruta Turística Severo Ochoa and the Northern Route (Asturia), which is also known as the Mtb Northern Route (Asturia). You might also encounter very short segments of local paths like San Antón de Concilleiro and Hoces del Esva I.
